name: LLM Respondent Distributions Vary by Model and Improve Unevenly With Ensembles
statement: Liu, Bhandari, and Pardos show that LLM respondent proficiency distributions and item-calibration correlations differ by model; no single LLM reproduces human respondent variability, while a resampled human-plus-LLM mixture improves Spearman correlation but yields mixed augmentation results.
population_or_context: College Algebra item-response simulation for item evaluation.
time_horizon: BJET 2025 article metadata and arXiv full text retrieved on 2026-06-02.
quantitative_note: Table 1 reports human SD 0.98 versus LLM SDs from 0.29 to 0.58; Table 2 reports GPT-3.5 Pearson 0.83/Spearman 0.87, GPT-4 Pearson 0.72/Spearman 0.78, and Experiment 3 Pearson 0.93/Spearman 0.93.
caveats: The item set is a single College Algebra lesson.
The ensemble/augmentation result improves rank correlation but does not eliminate concerns about distribution shift and RMSE.
evaluation_method: IRT proficiency distribution comparison, item-parameter calibration correlation, and augmentation/resampling experiments.
reported_metrics: Model-specific proficiency means and SDs, Pearson and Spearman item-calibration correlations, RMSE, and augmentation experiment correlations.
limitations: The evidence is multiple-choice College Algebra item-response evidence, not open-ended response realism.
No single model should be treated as a human substitute because LLM respondent distributions are too narrow.
The staged conclusion is about model and ensemble effects, not a recommendation to replace human item calibration.

source_locator.section: arXiv PDF Sections 3-5, Table 1, Table 2, and Discussion; ERIC publication metadata
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The staged finding paraphrases model-specific distribution and item-calibration results plus the ensemble/resampling conclusion.
